images.caption: Figure 1:Overview of 3DCodeBench.(Top Left)We explore the potential of Vision-Language Models to generate 3D objects via Procedural Code.(Bottom Left)The benchmark offers diverse, seed-addressable procedural test cases across a range of semantic categories.(Top Right)Qualitative comparisons of frontier VLMs (GPT-5.5, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Claude Opus 4.7) reveal varying capabilities in geometric reasoning.(Bottom Right)Alongside automated metrics,3DCodeArenaestablishes Elo rankings via pairwise human-preference evaluations.

header: 33DCodeBench: Towards 3D Generation via Writing Procedural Code

images.caption: Figure 2:Agentic data-curation pipeline.A VLM-driven agent leverages a structured knowledge base to transform complex procedural factories into standalone Blender Python scripts via API migration, geometric validation, and code simplification. Visual fidelity is maintained through an iterative refinement loop using multi-view renders. Finally, every generated(prompt, code, mesh)triplet undergoes stricthuman-in-the-loopverification to guarantee the highest benchmark quality.

Figure 3:3DCodeBench dataset statistics.(a) A semantic word cloud illustrating the diversity of the 212 curated categories. (b) Distribution of code-line counts per script (mean531531, median387387), highlighting the script complexity. (c) Distribution of file sizes per script (mean20.520.5KB, median14.914.9KB), with a long right tail driven by intricate geometry-node definitions.

Figure 4:Automated perception metrics vs. human preference.For four representative per-model quality metrics, SigLIP-2 view-paired (cond.), Executability, Uni3D 3D↔\leftrightarrow3D (cond.), and Chamfer Distance (cond.), we plot the value (at each model’s best thinking level, averaged across the text-to-3D and image-to-3D tracks) against the 3DCodeArena Elo on the1212evaluated VLMs and report Pearsonrrand Spearmanρ\rho. The Chamfer panel uses an inverted x-axis.

Figure 5:Single-turn ablations.Top row sweeps the thinking budget across all 12 VLMs on (a) text-to-3D and (b) image-to-3D, reported as SigLIP-2 view-paired (penalized) similarity. Bottom row sweeps the number of input reference viewsN∈{1,2,3,4}N\in\{1,2,3,4\}on the image-to-3D track for the six backbones (four Gemini and two Gemma) we have multi-view runs for, reported as (c) Uni3D 3D–3D (conditional) and (d) SigLIP-2 (conditional), matching the successful-output quality convention in TableA.1. Solid lines with±\pmstd error bars use 3-seed means where multiple seeds are available; markers without error bars are single-seed runs.

Figure 6:Qualitative comparisonof Gemini 3.1 Pro, Claude Opus 4.7, and GPT-5.5 against the 3DCodeBench reference on six prompts. Every mesh is the render of each model’s procedural output under the agentic workflow.

Figure A.1:Cost versus human-preference Elo across the1010paid frontier VLMs.Thexx-axis is per-query list-price cost (USD, log scale) and theyy-axis is 3DCodeArena Bradley–Terry Elo (higher is better;3,0983{,}098votes). Each point is labeled with its (cost / Elo) and the dashed line is the Pareto frontier. Four of the five frontier points are Gemini models, and GPT-5.5 is the only non-Gemini point on it.

Figure A.2:3DCodeArena Elo vs. all six automated quality metrics (best-level aggregation).The full six-panel version of the main-paper Figure4, which shows only four panels for space. Same per-model palette; per-panel Pearsonrrand Spearmanρ\rhoin the upper-left; the Chamfer panel uses an inverted x-axis so all panels read “better metric→\tohigher Elo”.

Figure A.3:3DCodeArena Elo vs. each automated quality metric, single-shot run.Same six-panel layout as Figure4but using each model’s single-shot base run (rather than best thinking level) averaged across both tracks. Per-panel Pearsonrrand Spearmanρ\rhoare reported in the upper-left; the Chamfer panel uses an inverted x-axis (smaller is better) so all panels read left-to-right as “better metric→\tohigher Elo”.

Figure A.4:Thinking-level ablation, complementary metrics.Top row: text-to-3D, with (a) Uni3D 3D–3D paired cosine and (b) Uni3D text–3D cross-modal cosine (the input prompt vs. the generated point cloud). Bottom row: image-to-3D, with (c) Uni3D 3D–3D paired cosine and (d) DINOv3 view-paired (facebook/dinov3-vitl16-pretrain-lvd1689m) cosine. All values are penalized means; error bars are1​σ1\sigmaacross33Gemini/Gemma seeds.

header: Appendix DIterative inference and multi-stage pipelines

images.caption: Figure E.1:3DCodeArena — Text-to-3D interface.Voters see the natural-language prompt at the top and two anonymized gray-shaded GLB renders side by side; both viewers are independently orbit-rotatable and zoomable, and the vote is cast with one of four buttons (A is better/B is better/Tie/Both bad). The pairing shown here is real (a perching-bird prompt; both meshes are the live exports our voters saw); model identities are revealed only after the vote.

Figure E.2:3DCodeArena — Image-to-3D interface.The same blind pairwise layout as FigureE.1, with the additional reference-image strip at the top: the four canonical views of the target object that the policy was conditioned on are rendered above the prompt so voters can score multi-view faithfulness directly against the input. The pairing shown here is a tube-coral prompt; voters again judge only the geometry.

header: Appendix FLLM/VLM-as-a-judge against the human arena

images.caption: Figure G.1:Quality vs. sampling temperature,33seeds per cell atthinking_level=high,1​σ1\sigmaerror bars across seeds.Top:text-to-3D (Exec, SigLIP-2 mean, SigLIP-2 max).Bottom:image-to-3D (Exec, SigLIP-2 mean, DINOv3 mean). Pro is largely flat forT∈[0,1.5]T\in[0,1.5]and only drops atT=2.0T{=}2.0; Flash is similarly robust; Flash Lite peaks sharply atT=0.7T{=}0.7and degrades aboveT=1.0T{=}1.0.T=2.0T{=}2.0is the worst configuration in every cell.
